title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee met on May 11, 2026 and approved several items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, approved construction agreements for Military Road utilities and Radkkey Avenue water/sewer connections, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines starting at $100/day.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkkey Avenue duplex
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Program grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project that will likely move to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the approximately $1.5 million project. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Updates were also provided on Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading, Schofield Avenue punch list items with HOS Construction, and Fuller Street restoration with Melvin. A special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue was scheduled for Thursday, May 28th at 6:00 PM.

Staff reported on spring activities including removing road limits on April 29th, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations along Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week, spring curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Storm damage from East Nick required tree cleanup. Culverts failed on Jones Street requiring installation of a third 18-inch pipe. Shop repairs included winterizing plow trucks, tractor and mower maintenance, and various equipment servicing. A new tandem axle chassis has been ordered with upfitter items for the first truck.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and private well sampling. Sanitary side experienced significant clear water infiltration issues with lift stations running overtime. Data showed 37 million gallons pumped into the water system in April but 53-54 million gallons treated at sanitary facilities - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified homes illegally discharging sump pumps into sanitary sewers. The INI study with in-pipe technologies will help identify problem areas. Water main flushing notices going out with work starting next week. Well 2 rehab at Carey started today.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing operating revenues of approximately $3 million, net operating income of $482,911, and water loss at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves 6,100 customers total with 5,250 in Weston and 830 in Rothschild. PAS settlement funds of $1.44 million were received. The committee moved to acknowledge the report and it passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term sustainable funding for state transportation, noting that current grant funding helps one-time costs but recurring annual payments for street maintenance and snow plowing remain underfunded. The committee moved to recommend village board adoption of Resolution 2026-014, which passed unanimously.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled material at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Analysis showed village cost of $17.11/cubic yard versus City of Wausau paying $28/cubic yard - saving approximately $130,000 across three 2026 projects. The committee unanimously approved awarding the contract to King Gravel.

Staff sought guidance on enforcing violations for illegal clear water discharge into sanitary sewers. Current ordinance allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee members discussed distinguishing between unknowing versus willful violations. Trustee Zagami suggested waiting for the INI study results, then sending violation letters with 30-60 day correction periods. The committee unanimously directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100 per day for first offenses.

Michael explained a builder wants to construct a duplex on lot 1 which currently lacks water/sewer main frontage. Staff proposed allowing a temporary long lateral connection until Radkkey Avenue is reconstructed, at which time the owner would pay assessment for main extension. Committee members requested adding a specific date, agreeing on 'no later than 2029.' Motion passed unanimously to recommend board approval with the added timeline.

Michael presented an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road reconstruction. Weston's share covers utility relocation at estimated $94,860 ($6,156 sanitary sewer, $88,704 water main). Steam Construction is the contractor. The water main requires relocation due to storm sewer conflicts. Motion passed unanimously to recommend board approval.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910 - MSA recommended. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730, but Michael recommended Clark Dietz for the extra $3,000 due to having experienced full-time staff rather than summer interns. Committee unanimously approved MSA for the roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel.

actionItems: Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Forward Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding to village board for adoption
Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Draft enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines starting at $100/day
Prepare water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline of no later than 2029
Forward Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild ($94,860 Weston share) to village board
Engage MSA for Alderson/Jelnik roundabout construction services ($37,315) and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue ($73,730)
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs in eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration impact for future meeting
Investigate street lighting options for Schofield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road as part of future reconstruction

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with all commissioners present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period for items not on the agenda
Minutes of April 13, 2026 planning commission meeting
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program - Wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to address challenges with a gas pipeline easement running through the property, with parking now located over the pipeline (acceptable to the gas company). Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond the current 40 officers. Discussion included an alternate storage shed placement to allow for future building expansion to the north. Commissioner Garant moved to approve the site plan subject to staff conditions 2-5. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program - Wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Jennifer presented a new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter inspection focused on material handling and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT requirements was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near the village well on Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process. Commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring that facility into compliance. A motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Motion to approve minutes as presented passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Jennifer announced the first meeting of the joint planning commission/village board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Discussion followed about residential TIF districts and workforce housing, with Jennifer noting Ehlers has a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via remote connection. He requested the planning commission use common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the new police department project to avoid excessive taxpayer costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and findings, noting some zoning complaints are years old.
actionItems: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan approved subject to staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection findings back to Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Joint planning commission/village board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Jennifer to send email and calendar event for June 1st steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging stations to be discussed at future meeting in fall
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational meeting to orient new board members on the departments under their jurisdiction. Department heads from the Health Department, Veterans Services, and Aging & Disability Resource Center presented overviews of their programs, budgets, and current initiatives. The committee approved the April 1st meeting minutes unanimously.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call noting remote attendees
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items presented
Educational presentations introduction and committee ordinance review
Next meeting date discussion and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all in favor.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's structure under section 2.04 subsection 5, noting they oversee the Health Department, Social Services, Veterans Services, and receive reports from North Central Health Care and the Aging & Disability Resource Center. He explained reporting relationships with statutory boards including the Social Services Board, Veterans Service Commission, and Board of Health.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's 37.6 FTE staff and four divisions. She highlighted public health vending machines stocked with free tick kits and other supplies, the Nurse Family Partnership program graduating its first two-year-olds, and a new homeless system response position. The department has approximately $5 million budget with about half from tax levy. Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness, and Suginaka confirmed the department has plans in place and staff trained in emergency response from COVID experience.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was absent. The department has nearly 140 FTE with a $22 million budget, about $7 million from tax levy. He highlighted the successful Family Keys program addressing housing instability, noting it was substantially changed based on board feedback. He mentioned potential restructuring requests may come for children's long-term support and other units due to increased need.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his 3.0 FTE office had 8,149 veteran interactions in 2025, up from approximately 5,900 the prior year. He noted $44 million in annual VA compensation flows to Marathon County veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. He highlighted partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues for housing and suicide prevention efforts, noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination, and Galindo explained state statute requires each county have a VSO but veterans can work with any office.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) with about 60 FTEs and 385-400 volunteers. He noted funding challenges with federal Older Americans Act not reauthorized in two years while nutrition costs increased 40-45% in four years. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135. The ADRC had not requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024. Vice Chair Rosenberg thanked the program, mentioning volunteering at the Hmong American Center meal site.
Vice Chair Rosenberg caught that the announced date of May 29th was incorrect as it's not a Wednesday. The committee confirmed the next meeting will be Tuesday, June 3rd at 3 PM.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare presentation to be scheduled for a separate full meeting
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggested future agenda items
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion to upcoming meetings
Next meeting scheduled for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and held extensive discussion about farmers market parking challenges at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, Pledge of Allegiance, Roll Call
Public Hearing on Park and Recreation Impact Fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing on Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented that there is no real data justifying the specific numbers. Trustee Clark supported the fee as justified given population increases. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance creates a reciprocal inspection agreement between Safer and municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others. Once a food truck is inspected by any participating department, a sticker is placed indicating compliance, eliminating the need for multiple inspections. Motion by Lopes, second by Zagami, approved unanimously.

Significant debate occurred over policy language. The board voted to strike sentences requiring members to explain health, safety, or welfare reasons for remote attendance, instead keeping the policy broad. Trustees Beck and Clark moved and seconded the amendment to strike the restrictive language. The amendment passed 5-2 with Hermling and Motti opposed. The original motion as amended also passed 5-2 with the same dissenting votes.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, approved unanimously. The board agreed to include committee applicant resumes in future packets.

discussions.item: Secondhand Article and Jewelry Dealer License
discussions.body: License approval for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street was discussed. Chief explained the license requirement relates to tracking secondhand merchandise to prevent stolen goods sales. The board amended the motion to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. Both the amendment and final motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: Extended discussion about parking concerns at the new Kennedy Park location after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield Avenue site to development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass. Trustee Clark noted historical parking on grass at parks for sporting events. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors now can keep vehicles with them. The board discussed but took no action, with some suggesting if parking issues cannot be resolved, the market may need to pause. Renee confirmed the market operates at no loss due to grants and reimbursements.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment, criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when the engineering technician position remains unfilled. He also criticized the process for appointing Lisa Beck to the Safer board, claiming she was introduced at a Fire Department meeting before official board appointment. He criticized lack of detailed Kennedy Park information in the packet and called for greater administrator accountability. Pitel also commented during the park impact fee public hearing questioning the justification for specific fee amounts.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective immediately per Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor reciprocal inspection ordinance adopted
E-bike and nuisance ordinance amendments adopted
Resolution 2026-014 supporting long-term transportation funding adopted
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove restrictive language about reasons for remote attendance
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC pending occupancy and background check
Committee applicant resumes to be included in future board packets
Comprehensive Plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 6:00 PM
Speed limit study for Weston Avenue to be brought back to the board
Staff to bring back intergovernmental service agreement for assisting Ringle with building inspections

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee at-large positions, and unanimously passed a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris following severe tornado and flood damage in the Ringle area. The board heard emotional testimony from affected residents requesting fee waivers.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Roll call and sign-in (technical difficulties with meeting manager)
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Ballot counting for Executive Committee election
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster relief

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspectives, developing new supervisors as leaders, and honoring supervisor preferences. Over 34% of the board is in their first or second term. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's youngest children and oldest elders. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.
Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: From Health and Human Services Committee and Human Resources and Finance and Property Committee. Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P [inaudible], seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill Tipping Fee Modification for Disaster Relief
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for storm debris following severe tornado and flood damage affecting Ringle and other communities. The standard $65/ton gate rate would be reduced to $36.94/ton by removing the state fee ($13.10), Ringle's local fee ($2.33), and reducing to the commercial contract rate. Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck were granted permission to speak, requesting Marathon County waive all fees as the state and town of Ringle have done. Board members raised questions about timelines, geographic scope, and long-term policy. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to bring the resolution directly from the Solid Waste Management Board. The resolution then passed unanimously on motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty, a Ringle resident, requested Marathon County waive all fees for six months, noting businesses have donated up to $40,000 in cleanup efforts and arguing the county should contribute like other entities. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, noting approximately 44 homes were affected and many are still waiting for insurance adjusters, requesting more time for fee relief.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Reduced landfill tipping fees ($36.94/ton) authorized through June 17, 2025 for certified storm debris
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for uninsured debris disposal costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term disaster debris policy within six months
Board to revisit tipping fee relief at May meeting if extension beyond state declaration period is needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will waive only septic system reconnect fees (not other zoning or pouts fees) for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried with no opposition on a voice vote.

discussions.item: Rezoning petition - Tim Veland on behalf of James Offer and David Tract
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential in the Town of Green Valley. The Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for or against. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Rezoning petition - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture in the Town of McMillan. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained it currently has a home and horse riding stable, and the rezoning would make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. He confirmed no natural area would be lost. The Town Board recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather damaged properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and pouts fees for properties damaged by the tornado affecting the towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about tracking costs and potential levy impacts. County Administrator Lance clarified this wouldn't require a budget amendment. Supervisor Drebeck questioned fairness to individual tornado victims who wouldn't qualify. After discussion, Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the resolution to the county board. Supervisor Drebeck then offered an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees for pouts systems ($160 each), excluding all other zoning and pouts fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution then passed on a non-unanimous voice vote to forward to the county board.

Director David Hagenbusher provided an overview of the department's operations, including the landfill facility operating since 1980, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy that began injecting gas in September 2025, and upcoming challenges including a $6 million 11-acre expansion and required leachate treatment facility by 2032. He noted the department applied for clean water fund financing but was asked to reapply for 2027 funding. Marilyn Bend praised the department's work and competitive influence on private landfills.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees serving both city and county park systems. He highlighted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as his replacement.

actionItems: Rezoning from urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential for James Offer and David Tract property in Town of Green Valley approved - forwarded to County Board
Rezoning from conservancy recreation to general agriculture for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property in Town of McMillan approved - forwarded to County Board
Modified fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) for April 17, 2026 tornado-damaged properties forwarded to County Board for consideration
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers
Solid Waste to reapply for clean water fund financing by October 2025 for 2027 funding cycle

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, recommending approval of amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances, an e-bike ordinance prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, fire inspection requirements for mobile food vendors, address corrections, and a raze order for an abandoned structure. The committee also discussed but deferred action on overnight parking regulations pending staff analysis.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledge department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Overnight parking regulations October 31st to May 1st
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: Roman Kratcha reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sold at auction and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt noted the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day) and 410 traffic stops with 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

Committee confirmed previous changes including switching tree limb height requirements to 14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways, and adding language requiring compliance with jurisdictions having authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion focused on emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. The committee debated whether to restrict electric scooters, ultimately deciding officer discretion would apply. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with the addition that emotos are prohibited from operating on roadways, sidewalks, trails, or any public property in the Village of Weston passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4 AM to 4 PM) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities, which typically have 5-hour windows. Michael Wodalski from Public Works noted most plowing doesn't begin until 4-5 AM and suggested targeting specific problem areas with signage. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight parking entirely, preferring uniform restrictions that allow officers time to clear vehicles before plows arrive. Committee deferred action, directing Michael Wodalski to identify specific problem areas for the July meeting.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest High School parking lot. Staff estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000, while a lighted system would be $25,000-$30,000. Committee members expressed reluctance to set precedent for village-funded crosswalks upon request. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the cost, the committee recommends board approval. Motion passed unanimously. Michael Wodalski will contact Mount Olive.

Chief Hunt explained a regional partnership where fire departments from Weston, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Clover, Riverside, and Wisconsin Rapids would mutually recognize food truck fire inspections. Fire Marshal Marty already inspected one truck that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Wausau opted not to participate, preferring their own system. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Jen Higgins presented three addressing corrections needed for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicowski Street (duplex needing two addresses), properties on Ross Avenue that were out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue near a new duplex development. Chief Hunt added Weston Villa Apartments on Delicowski also has addressing issues with eight apartments having separate addresses. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees.

Staff presented a raze request for an abandoned structure that has been vacant since 2010-2011 and on the code enforcement list since 2021. A previous fire department training burn was cancelled due to unsafe structural conditions. Chickens were once discovered being housed in the structure. Demolition costs will be placed on the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend authorization passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke expressing concerns about the new farmers market location, predicting grass damage from vehicle parking. He questioned whether parking on grass violates village code and suggested ADA-compliant handicap spaces should be on asphalt rather than grass. He also questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks and suggested long-standing code violations on the enforcement list need better accountability and progress reports.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisances amendments to Board of Trustees
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition to Board of Trustees
Michael Wodalski to identify specific areas needing parking restrictions for July meeting
Michael Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost-sharing
Adopt Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend address corrections to Board of Trustees
Recommend raze order authorization for 4104 Shore Avenue to Board of Trustees
Staff to investigate property condition complaint at Sternberg and South Timber

title: Tourism Commission
overview: Based on the published agenda, this special meeting of the Village of Weston Tourism Commission was scheduled to elect new officers and consider a $10,000 grant request for the Taste N Glow Balloon Fest. The meeting was set to address leadership positions for the commission and evaluate funding for a major annual event expected to draw 15,000 attendees to the area.

agenda.item: Election of Officers: Chair, Vice Chair, Secretary
Approval of minutes from the previous meeting: April 21, 2026
Grant Request: Taste N Glow Fest

discussions.body: The commission was scheduled to elect leadership positions including Chair, Vice Chair, and Secretary. Current commission members eligible for these positions include Ermeling, Frederick, Hodell, Jalowitz, and Zeyghami.

discussions.item: Grant Request: Taste N Glow Fest
discussions.body: The commission was set to consider a $10,000 room tax grant request from Taste N Glow Balloon Fest for their annual two-day event scheduled for July 10-11, 2026 in Marathon City. The event expects approximately 15,000 attendees and 45 estimated room nights, with funds to be used for advertising, potential shuttles, and lodging expenses for balloon pilots and crew members.

publicComment: Public comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on election of Chair, Vice Chair, and Secretary
Expected to consider approval of April 21, 2026 meeting minutes
Scheduled to vote on $10,000 grant request for Taste N Glow Balloon Fest

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Village of Weston Finance & Human Resource Committee was scheduled to meet on May 18, 2026 to review April financial reports including budget status and TIF district details, as well as receive a presentation on Clerk's Department operations and budget. This routine monthly meeting was set to provide fiscal oversight of village finances and departmental operations.

agenda.item: Approval of Minutes from Previous Finance & HR Meeting: April 21, 2026
TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
Discussion on Clerk's Department Operations and Budget

discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to acknowledge the April 2026 financial report for all funds. According to the attached report, general fund revenue stood at 70.3% of budget while expenditures were at 40.7% of budget, with staff monitoring several line items that may exceed their budgets.

discussions.item: TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
discussions.body: The committee was set to review the monthly Tax Increment Financing district reports for TIF 1 and TIF 2. These reports provide ongoing monitoring of the village's tax increment financing districts.
The committee was expected to acknowledge the legal details report for April, providing oversight of the village's legal expenditures.

discussions.item: Discussion on Clerk's Department Operations and Budget
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to receive an educational presentation on Clerk's Department operations and budget. This follows a similar presentation format from the previous month when Planning & Development operations were reviewed.

publicComment: Public comments section was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on approval of April 21, 2026 meeting minutes
Expected to acknowledge April Budget Status Report
Expected to acknowledge TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
Expected to acknowledge Legal Details for April

title: Parks Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Parks and Recreation Committee was scheduled to meet on May 26, 2026 to receive updates on Kennedy Park and the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course, and to consider action on Dog Day at the Aquatic Center, graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail, and kayak launch signage. The meeting included several community recreation facility matters relevant to Weston residents.

agenda.item: Approval of minutes from the previous meeting: April 27, 2026
Discussion and possible action Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
Discussion and possible action of Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Discussion and possible action Yellowbanks Kayak Launch Signs

discussions.body: Director Osterbrink was scheduled to provide an update on Kennedy Park developments. Based on previous meeting minutes, this was expected to include information on the capital campaign, grants, and ongoing renovation work.
The committee was set to receive an update on the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course. This was expected to cover current status and any developments related to the facility.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to discuss and potentially take action on holding a Dog Day event at the Aquatic Center. This type of event typically allows dogs to swim at the pool at the end of the summer season.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: The committee was expected to consider how to address graffiti issues on the Eau Claire River Trail. Discussion was scheduled to potentially include response measures or prevention strategies.
The committee was set to discuss and potentially approve signage for the Yellowbanks Kayak Launch. This follows previous discussions about the launch facility and its amenities.

publicComment: Public Comments and Written Comments were both listed as agenda items.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on approval of April 27, 2026 meeting minutes
Expected to consider action on Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
Expected to consider action regarding graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Expected to consider action on Yellowbanks Kayak Launch Signs

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

agenda.item: Consideration of the minutes of the preceding meeting(s) - April 8, 2026 Regular Wausau Water Works Commission Minutes
Director's Reports - Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
Director's Reports - Wastewater Project Updates: Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station and Lift Station Assessment w/ Clark Dietz
Approving the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects (CIP) Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Approving the Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control (CCC) Program
Approving the Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners
Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

actionItems: Implement 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Execute sole source renewal agreement with HydroCorp for Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program
Proceed with sole source procurement of MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers for wastewater treatment
Next meeting scheduled for June 2, 2026 at 11:00 AM

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
